sig: resolve(query: string | ResolveQuery, opts?: RequestOptions): Promise<IdentityResolution>
desc: Resolve any identifier to a canonical IdentityResolution. Identity is the hard problem in music data — the same artist is a Bandcamp subdomain, a Discogs id, an MBID and five spellings. This collapses any of them to crate's single canonical `cluster_id`, plus every other place that artist lives online. It's the first call in most workflows, because almost everything else is keyed on `cluster_id`. Accepts a bare string (inferred: URL → `url`, `discogs:`/`mbid:` → locator, 64-hex → `cluster`, else → `q`) or an explicit one-of object. A 200 with `cluster_id: null` is an honest gap, not an error.

Fetch an artist dossier in one call — the shortcut when you already mean a specific artist. Hand it almost anything (a name, slug, 64-hex `cluster_id`, or `discogs:`/`mbid:` locator): a direct key hits `/artist/{key}`, while a locator or bare numeric id is resolved first. An unresolved locator throws CrateNotFoundError — use artistOrNull to receive `null`.
throws: when a locator/numeric id resolves to no cluster.

sig: artistOrNull(key: string, opts?: RequestOptions): Promise<ArtistDossierContract | null>
desc: Like artist, but returns `null` for the honest-gap case (a locator or numeric id that resolves to no cluster) instead of throwing. crate is honest about absence — reach for this in pipelines and agent loops where 'not found' is a normal branch, not an exception to catch.
example:
const a = await crate.artistOrNull('discogs:999999'); // → null if unresolved
if (a) console.log(a.display);

desc: Faceted discovery across the whole catalogue. Filter by genre, style, format, country, label and year range — each multi-value facet combines with AND or OR via its `*_mode` sibling. The response also tells you whether the total count is exact or estimated from a sample.
example:
const hits = await crate.search({ genre: ['idm', 'ambient'], genre_mode: 'or', year_from: 2000, limit: 20 });
// → hits.results: ResultRow[]; hits.pagination.total_results / total_results_mode

desc: The emerging-artists breakouts index — artists breaking out now, backed by corroborating evidence (press, bookings) rather than a single noisy signal. Each item carries an emergence tier, a score, and how it was corroborated.
example:
const b = await crate.breakouts();
b.items.forEach((i) => console.log(i.name, i.emergenceTier, i.corroboration));

desc: The self-describing API root — a machine-readable map of every resource plus a 'cold start' recipe. The one **keyless** endpoint, so a new integration or an agent can discover what's possible before committing to anything.

A precomputed facet snapshot — the available filter values and their counts. Call it before building a search UI (or an agent query) so you filter by values that actually return results. **Key-gated.**
example: const facets = await crate.facets(); // requires apiKey
throws: on a non-2xx response (401/402 if the key lacks access).

desc: Enrich one 'master' (a record/album in the Discogs sense) with crate's behavioral signals: collector ownership, DJ play counts, critic attention, formats, and its slot in the cube taxonomy — the per-record intelligence layer over plain discography. **Key-gated.** `id` is a positive integer (server-validated).
example:
const m = await crate.master(1234567); // requires apiKey
// → m.owner_count, m.dj_count, m.cube_quadrant, …

desc: Batch master enrichment — up to 100 records in one round-trip. Returns the rows it found plus an explicit `not_found` list, so a partial result is first-class rather than all-or-nothing. Use it to hydrate a page of search results in one call instead of N. **Key-gated.**
example:
const batch = await crate.masters([12345, 67890]); // requires apiKey
// → batch.results: MasterEnrichment[]; batch.not_found: number[]

desc: Your meter — plan tier, calls made this month, quota and remaining, and the per-minute burst limit. Poll it to render a usage bar or back off before a wall. **Key-gated.**
example:
const u = await crate.usage(); // requires apiKey
console.log(`${u.calls_this_month}/${u.quota_monthly} on ${u.tier}`);

desc: The per-artist Bandcamp feed — the bridge from an identity to its Bandcamp footprint. `artistKey` is a cluster_id, `discogs:<id>`, or `mbid:<uuid>`.
example: const feed = await crate.bandcamp('discogs:1234');

desc: One keyset page of crate's platform-wide Bandcamp signal feed (the whole feed, not one artist). Each page returns an opaque `next_cursor` you persist to resume — use this when you manage paging yourself across runs or processes.
example:
const page = await crate.bandcamp.bulk({ source: 'signals_mbid' });
// → page.rows: Record<string, unknown>[]; page.next_cursor: 'eyJ…' | null

desc: The same feed, paged for you: an async iterable that follows cursors automatically until the feed ends. Yields rows by default; call `.pages()` for whole pages (with `_meta`), and cap the sweep with `maxPages`.
The no-arg Bandcamp index — discover the valid `source` names for `bulk()` / `bulkAll()` rather than guessing the string.
example: const idx = await crate.bandcamp.index();

sig: release(query: { item: string } | { url: string }, opts?: RequestOptions): Promise<BandcampRelease | null>
desc: Zoom into one Bandcamp album and get its dossier, including the full tracklist, by opaque item id or album URL. Returns `null` on the honest gap (HTTP 200 `present: false`) — not an error. `track.track_url` is the track PAGE, never a playable stream (those are tokenised/ToS-bound); artwork is link-only.

sig: releases(query: { clusterId: string }, opts?: RequestOptions): Promise<BandcampReleaseSummary[]>
desc: All of an artist's Bandcamp releases for a `cluster_id` (usually from `resolve()`) as lightweight summary rows — no tracklists. List a discography fast, then `release()` into the one you want.
example: const releases = await crate.bandcamp.releases({ clusterId });

desc: The full master (record) dossier — richer than master's flat enrichment: a structured header, per-field sections, artwork, freshness, provenance.
example: const d = await crate.dossier.master(1234567);

desc: The deepest grain crate offers: an artist profile from ~24 independent facets (emergence, live demand, tastemaker support, journalism, network position…), each section present-with-signals or honestly marked absent. crate's flagship.
example:
const d = await crate.dossier.artist('four-tet');
// → d.emergence.signals?.emergenceTier, d.live_demand.signals?.demandTier, …

desc: The label-grain dossier by slug.
example: const d = await crate.dossier.label('warp-records');

desc: The festival-grain dossier by slug (identity + lineup signals) — useful for tying artists to the events that book them.
example: const d = await crate.dossier.festival('dekmantel');

desc: A table of contents for the dossier system — which grains exist, which are available, and the contract version you're coding against.
example:
const m = await crate.dossier.manifest();
console.log(m.contract_version, m.grains);

desc: The tastemaker leaderboard — early-moving curators/DJs ranked, with ones-to-watch and early-spinner slices. Leading indicators, not vanity counts.
example:
const t = await crate.tastemakers();
t.leaderboard.forEach((x) => console.log(x.rank, x.name, x.ownTier));

desc: Just the 'ones to watch' slice — rising names with emergence and momentum tiers.
example: const w = await crate.tastemakers.onesToWatch();

desc: Ask the catalogue a question in plain English and get an answer — the NL front door, handy when wiring crate into a chat or an agent.
example: const a = await crate.wayfind('breakout dub techno artists from 2023');

desc: The 'show your work' twin of `wayfind()`: returns the structured search params it understood from your text, ready to hand to search. **Key-gated.**
example: const params = await crate.wayfind.interpret('ambient from japan, 90s');

sig: observed(body: ObservedBeaconInput, opts: RequestOptions & { beaconToken: string }): Promise<void>
desc: Report an observed search event (e.g. a cache hit). Returns nothing on success — fire-and-acknowledge telemetry.

sig: refined(body: RefinedBeaconInput, opts: RequestOptions & { beaconToken: string }): Promise<void>
desc: Report a refined search — which facets changed (from → to) — so crate learns how people narrow results.
